figures.caption: Figure 7: Failure modes. Severe depth estimation error produces wrong warping, leads to bad camera control.
Figure 9: More comparison with state-of-the-art methods on Davis.

Figure 6: Top canonical partial correlation ρ1(R̂βγ) on the model’s clean prediction ẑ0(zt, t, c) along the H , W , L axes (left to right) at five noise levels. The diagonal band stays sharp at every σt with off-diagonal mass concentrated within |β − γ|≤2, confirming that the locality structure of Figure 2 is preserved across the sampling schedule.
Table 1: Visual quality and trajectory-error results on RealEstate10K. Best across all methods in bold, second-best underlined. † marks training-based methods.
Figure 3: Qualitative results on RealEstate10K. Compared with the baselines, our method generates photorealistic novel views that align with the target camera poses.
Table 2: Trajectory-error and visual quality results on DAVIS. Best across all methods in bold, second-best underlined. † marks training-based methods.
Figure 4: Qualitative results on DAVIS. On dynamic scenes, h-control achieves accurate camera control and good visual quality simultaneously, while baselines trade one for the other.

Figure 1: 2D checkerboard toy example. (a) Sample clouds at yobs≈0.5 for ground truth, DPS [11], TFG-UGD [12], and h-control (left to right). (b) Posterior-hit rate vs. total NFE for h-control (varying Jmax) and TFG-UGD (varying Nrecur). (c) |∆(j) W | vs. inner iteration j binned by noise band.

Table 5: Inference speed of training-free and training-based camera-control baselines and ours, reported at each method’s native operating resolution on a single NVIDIA A40 GPU. † marks training-based methods.
Figure 2: Top canonical partial correlation ρ1(R̂βγ) along the H , W , L axes of the Wan 2.2 latent (N=200 encoded videos). Off-diagonal mass concentrates within |β − γ|≤2.
